title: To check whether assessment is provisional before issuing SCN or passing adjudication order
text:
Circular No. 298/14/97-CX

dated 25/2/97

F.No. 199/118/96-JC (BMB)

Government of India

Ministry of Finance

Department of Revenue

Central Board of Excise & Customs, New Delhi

Subject: Assessments whether provisional need for verification before issuing Show Cause Notice and passing the
adjudication order -Reg.

I am directed to say that a case has come to the notice of the Board wherein a Show Cause Notice was issued for demanding
duty for a period of five years and the Commissioner while adjudicating the case held that the demand was time barred as
there was no suppression of facts etc. The Commissioner"s order was sent for review to the Board. Subsequent enquiries
made by Board revealed that the assessments in the case were provisional and, therefore, the question of limitation of time
could not arise in this case. It appears that this mistake had occurred since all relevant facts were not mentioned in the Show
Cause Notice and the same were not ascertained at the time of adjudication of the case against the revenue even though the
revenue involved was very substantial.

2.    The Board desires that the concerned officers should take appropriate note of the above instance to ensure that such
instances do occur.

Sd/-

(M.K. Gupta)

Director (Review)
gemini_reasoning: This chunk is a Central Excise circular that clarifies a crucial point: the normal time limits for demanding duty do not apply when assessments are provisional. It instructs officers to verify the status of an assessment before issuing a Show Cause Notice to avoid incorrectly dropping demands as time-barred.

questions.q: Can the department issue a Show Cause Notice for a Central Excise demand related to a period over five years ago if the assessments for that period were provisional?
questions.why_this_chunk: This circular explicitly states that in a case involving provisional assessments, 'the question of limitation of time could not arise'.

questions.q: We received a Show Cause Notice where the department overlooked that our assessments were provisional and we want to argue that the demand is time-barred. Is there any CBEC instruction that highlights this as a potential error by the department?
questions.why_this_chunk: This circular details a specific instance where an adjudicating authority wrongly held a demand to be time-barred because the provisional nature of the assessment was not ascertained, and directs officers to prevent such mistakes.

title: Customs Act, 1962
text:

be, may also be published if, in the opinion of the Central Government, circumstances of the case justify it.]

1. Inserted by the Taxation Laws (Amendment) Act, 2006 (29 of 2006), sec. 33 (w.e.f. 13.7.2006).

1 [Section 154AC . -

The Board may notify a common portal, to be called the Common Customs Electronic Portal, for facilitating registration, filing of
bills of entry, shipping bills, other documents and forms prescribed under this Act or under any other law for the time being in
force or the rules or regulations made thereunder, payment of duty and for such other purposes, as the Board may, by
notification, specify.]

1. Inserted (w.e.f. 28-03-2021) s. 100 of Finance Act 2021 (13 of 2021).

Section 155. Protection of action taken under the Act. -

(1) No suit, prosecution or other legal proceedings shall lie against the Central Government or any officer of the Government or a
local authority for anything which is done, or intended to be done in good faith, in pursuance of this Act or the rules or regulations.

(2) No proceeding other than a suit shall be commenced against the Central Government or any officer of the Government or a
local authority for anything purporting to be done in pursuance of this Act without giving the Central Government or such officer a
gemini_reasoning: This chunk provides the statutory basis for the Common Customs Electronic Portal (Section 154AC) and outlines the legal protection granted to government officers for actions taken in good faith under the Customs Act (Section 155).

questions.q: What is the statutory provision that empowers the Board to create a single common portal, like ICEGATE, for all customs filings and duty payments?
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 154AC explicitly authorizes the Board to notify a 'Common Customs Electronic Portal' for filing bills of entry, shipping bills, and paying duty.

questions.q: A customs officer detained my consignment based on a genuine but mistaken belief that the goods were restricted, causing me financial loss. Can I sue the officer personally for damages?
questions.why_this_chunk: Section 155(1) states that no suit or legal proceeding can be initiated against a government officer for anything done in good faith in pursuance of the Customs Act.
